answer:
**The Old English Elegies** (particularly chapter 14, *Widsith*) contains an explicit catalogue of named figures. This poem features a wandering scop (poet) who recites his travels among numerous tribes, peoples, and rulers, naming over 70 kings, heroes, and groups such as Eormanric (king of the Ostrogoths), the Myrgings, Hreth-king, and many others (e.g., "From the Myrgings his lineage arose... the home of Eormanric, the wrathful treaty-breaker").[^1][^2] It is one of the most famous "catalogue poems" in Old English literature, structured as a roll-call of historical and legendary figures across Europe and beyond.[^3]

No other works in the corpus contain comparably explicit, extended lists or catalogues of named figures (e.g., kings, sages/rishis, or heroes). Several Dharmaśāstras (Smṛti texts) like *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, *Manusmṛti*, *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, and *Viṣṇu Smṛti* discuss lineages (*gotra*s), types of sons, sages, or tribes in the abstract or numerically (e.g., "*gotra*s with one, two... or eleven sages"), but they do not provide named enumerations.[^4][^5][^6] Searches for terms like "list", "catalogue", "kings of", "sages", "rishi", "genealogy", and "gotras" surfaced scattered proper names or genealogical concepts, but no dedicated lists.[^7]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch.14 (*Widsith*), opening.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3]
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, work-level wiki card (distinctive phrasing on tribes/peoples).[/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/wiki/_work.card.md]
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch.14 wiki card (core theme of journeying "through most tribes and peoples").[/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/wiki/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac.card.md]
[^4]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, ch.1 (e.g., "*gotra*s" with numbered sages; no names listed).[/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-e5d627]
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, work wiki (e.g., tribes like "Matsyas, Panchalas"; Brahmarishis mentioned, not catalogued).[/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/wiki/_work.card.md]
[^6]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, ch.1 (e.g., "Thirteen sons... stated by Manu"; types, not named individuals).[/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/]
[^7]: Corpus search results for "kings", "sages", "rishi", "sons of", "lineage", "gotras" (Indic Smṛtis dominant but non-explicit).
